They they knew that, like, of course, like we couldn't be happier for you. Like, thank God you're not with your ex. I mean, she she didn't have a high bar to to jump. I mean, she probably tripped over that bar. It was so low with how my ex treated me. How did your understanding of yourself change after you came out? Understanding of myself. It was scary. It's. It took a long time to really know who I was. The first step is kind of realizing and internalizing, like, okay, I'm different. The things that I'm feeling is not what everybody else feels. And I acknowledge that. And then the second step is letting everybody else understand and acknowledge those things. But I'm still like going through the process. Everything that I did was new and scary and intimidating. Um, and it's always the thought that comes to the back of my head is, is this safe for me? Uh, people can be cruel. A little bit less in Spokane. I feel like Spokane was more liberal than. Than Boise. Even though Spokane is. A smaller. Town. Well, it's not the same. But as far as Washington goes, Washington is typically more liberal. Spokane is more on the conservative side of Washington, but still more liberal than Boise. And you have worked not just in Boise proper, but in some cities outside of Boise. Did you always feel like you could bring your whole self? No. So my first job in Boise was with an outpatient orthopedic clinic, and I worked in Emmett. I could not be myself to my patients. My coworkers knew. They were very supportive, but they would not, like, offer to patients like, yeah, she has a wife, you know, or her fiance is a woman. Uh, you know, and I'd be wearing, like, a ring. People would ask me, like Emmett. People are very, like, personable. Like it's a cowboy town. And they asked me, like, what does your husband do? And I would have to pause and like do a safety calculation in my head of, do I tell this person that I have a wife? Most of the time it was no, because I didn't feel safe. You know, gay marriage had just been legalized. And there's many people in Idaho that thought it was still illegal. I get asked all the time, oh my gosh, like, is that legal? Can you do that? I guess. Okay. Which is crazy to me because I was with the man for 11 years in a horrible relationship and that was okay. But being with somebody that I love, even if they're the same sex, was not okay in many people's eyes and I would hear it all the time in the clinic. People would be very homophobic. God, I don't feel safe here. So federally and for the whole country. Um, same sex marriage became legal in 2015, and there were several years before that where it felt like every three months there was a new a new state has a new law, right? And the tides were kind of turning in the country. What was it like, like living through that period of change and did it feel uncertain? And then this is also right around the time when you're looking to get married. Yeah. When my wife proposed to me, it was. You think it was February? It was Valentine's Day of 2015. Uh, and I can't remember the month that it was legalized nationally, but, uh, we weren't sure, like, where we were going to get married. I was assuming Washington, uh, but legally, like, was Idaho going to recognize our marriage? I don't think so. Idaho spent an exorbitant amount of money when it was nationally legalized to try to block that. I think it was like $1 million. Uh, and then to this day, they've made, um, like, try to make legislation where there'd be a trigger law, uh, if it was overturned from the Supreme Court that they would not have it be legal. Um, in Idaho again. If it if, uh, if same sex marriage had should not become legal in Idaho. Did you have a plan? We would get married in Washington and have. To live in Idaho. Which I don't know what that would have looked like. Are you ready to switch gears in a very big way? Yes. Ready. What inspired you to join Junior League of Boise? That's a that's a great question. Uh, let me think. So it was I joined in 2018, I believe. I can't remember when I was a provisional. If it was like at the end of 2017 into 18, um, I think it was the. Must have been the spring class. And my friend Anna Lori actually Invited me to join. She'd been in it for two years at that point, and she was talking about the league and was friends with Lorena. So that's how I met her. Uh, and she just had so many wonderful things to say about this organization, and it didn't take much to convince me. Um, I had been looking for an organization to join. I was the president of the PTA club when I was in school. Uh, I loved being involved in volunteering. And coming from my upbringing, I just wanted to make a difference. And I wanted to meet new people. And I'd never been in an organization with women, um, because I was always hanging out with the guys. Right. Mhm. And I remember her asking me to join and I'm like, well just take my money like here's a check. Like where do I sign up? Um, I didn't even know Like what I was getting into at that point. Can you tell me about your professional experience? Yeah. Uh, our professional class, I believe, started with about nine members, and I was the person who just didn't talk. I like don't don't it's like, don't make me raise my hand. I'm not going to offer anything. Uh, I'm just here to watch and listen. Uh, by the end of that provisional cohort, there was three of us. Okay. And our provisional project, it was basically just given to us because they could see, like, this provisional class is getting smaller and smaller. You just better give them something to do. So, uh, at the time, we were doing volunteer a thon as a league, and our job was to create the after party for all the volunteers. Um, tears after the volunteer thon. So me and my two other cohort members, uh, got, uh, a restaurant that was able to give us a pretty good deal and we got a little party for them. And that was our provisional project at the time. What is volunteer? One volunteer thon, um, is where we we have done in eight days, and the junior league, where I've done in the days where members of the league or friends go to, uh, like another organization or a cause, and we put in volunteer hours. So volunteer thon is when you have several of those happening at once, um, on the same day. And it takes a lot of coordination and effort to find the organizations that need help at the same day, at the same time, and then get all the volunteers organized to do that. And so big. portion of the league is all volunteering with other nonprofits all in one day. Yes. And for that year, I believe we had three organizations that we volunteered for on the same day. I couldn't tell you what they were, though. Okay. What's what? I know you can't recall what the volunteer projects were, but what would you say would be a typical volunteer project for a done in a day or for volunteerism? Yeah. Uh, so there's always the food bank, like, that's that's one that's pretty common. Um, there's Ronald McDonald House. So it's making food, um, for the people who are staying there. There's the zoo. Um, there's the Boise Art Museum. Um. There's, Uh, I can't remember the other ones. But in the very next year, I don't know if you're going to be asking this question for my next year. I was on projects and we were doing volunteers on again. And remember I said I didn't like to talk. Nobody even knew that I probably existed. But I was committed to do something and do something in a big way. So when the the chair of the projects team was saying, okay, we're going to do volunteer athon, like, let's make a task for everybody and we're out having dinner. And they got to the end of the table and they're like, lady, what do you want to do? I'm like, God, I want to run the whole thing. And they all look at me and they're like, what? You're like, yeah, I want to do that. So that year I doubled our volunteer fund, like, uh. Like done in a daze. Like I had six different organizations that we went to. And uh, and I helped to organize the after party, um, in providing, uh, free food for the volunteers. Uh, and we got, uh, brewery to host us as well. So I think that was the start of my leadership with me, like jumping out of my skin. I remember going to my car after that dinner when I said I would do everything, and I, like, almost had an existential crisis. Like, what did I just do to myself? Did you did you surprise yourself in that moment, or had you been. Yeah. Gearing up to raise your hand? Um. I think I just did it. Like, I mean, if you're probably looking at me, I was probably, like, going back and forth, like, am I going to do this? And yeah, I did it. It was a great learning experience. Um, the, the, the lessons I learned from doing that and the initiative that I showed was. It was seen by the leaders because when it was time to be chosen for like management team chairs or co-chairs, they put my name in and I was a co-chair of Touch a Truck the very next year. What is touch? Touch a truck is both a community, um, like Stem opportunity for for the community to come in and uh, like in the name, we would get big rig trucks for kids to climb on. See the people who would interact with those vehicles. You know, we had, uh, dump trucks and, uh, like, big hauler trucks and, um, fire trucks and, like, lots of other equipment. Uh, and the kids just had a great time, uh, climbing over all those things and that, you know, the parents would come and, and be able to, to do something with their kids. And it was also a fundraiser. So it was kind of both fundraiser and Stem for children. And it was a several hour long event. Yes, it was rain or shine the very last year we did it. It was a horribly rainy day, uh, unfortunately. And I think that was to have been like our fifth or sixth year doing it. And after that, they were deciding to sunset the truck just because I kind of ran its course. Um. Did you you were the co-chair. Was that in the last year they did it, or was that. Uh, I was planning on being chair the next year, but then there's like a hitch in this storyline because that was a transition in 2019 into 2020. Okay. And you can imagine what happened in 2020. I wasn't even going to ask that. But how did how did 20. I should have asked that. How did 2020 change league life? What happened in 2020? Oh my gosh. Wow. Well, the world changed and so did the league. I think we all had to pivot in a big way and consider what volunteer meant for us and like, uh, leadership and how are we going to navigate a world where we can't be physically close and still impact our community in a meaningful way and still fundraise. Can you imagine fundraising? Because I was on the fundraising team that year too. I was a co-chair of our advancement team, which sun setting touch a truck. Then we launched advancement. So you launched a fundraising team which was called advancement in 2020. And what on earth did you do to fundraise? We had to get creative. We tried various strategies to like, can we get a grant? Can we get money, um, from like state or government agencies? Um, can we do like we ended up doing a raffle? Um, I don't think it was that year. I think it was the following year when I was chair. We did a raffle. But yeah, during Covid, um, it was really challenging to, to raise any money. And, um, considering what we worked with and also since we weren't doing anything, there wasn't a lot of overhead of, you know, sending women to conferences, um, and doing all the things that the league would normally do in a day to day aspect. So our overhead dropped that year. So our fundraising wasn't as critical as years prior. Oh, and we were doing something called project 100. So we were giving away $10,000 a year to a past project, um, organization. Uh, so that was probably the biggest, uh, lift, um, on a monetary standpoint that we had to consider. Um. So in um, 2019 and 2020, where there are no in-person Junior league meetings or events. It was all virtual for. Yeah, the first year everything was virtual. Uh, whenever we would meet in person, it'd be like masked up outside, like in a white space. We could see each other in our eyes, but we, like, you know, wouldn't interact. It was a very interesting time to to be alive. Do you recall, um, who the project 100 partner was during that time? Um, I think I think in 2019 it was family advocates, and then 2020 it was the Idaho State Historical Society. and that year we actually only gave them $5,000 because of our deficit. Because of concerns about expenses. Mhm. Um, and tell me you told me a little bit about project 100. We're, we're reinvesting in the community for ten years. But. Explain it a little bit more. Yeah. So this project came about um, after like during our 90th year, uh, as we were looking forward to year 100 and at the time, you know, my provisional class, we had three people. We had around 35 active members years prior to me joining. Our membership was decreasing every single year. And they, the actives and the Sustainers were very concerned about the longevity of the league, what our signature project is going to be. Um, you know, we had a pretty robust investment account, um, built up that we could pull from. And their idea leading up into our 100th year so we can have a bit of a runway to figure out our next project and hopefully increase our membership during that time is, uh, created project 100, meaning that every year leading up to 100 years. So ten years, we were looking back into our rich history and past building up community, uh, you know, our partnerships and organizations in the community and bringing them back and saying we had a part in your history. Let's talk about this and reconnect that relationship. And give them $10,000 as a thank you. All right. So. You, uh, have come to the role of president. In the 2025, 20, 26 year. Tell me how you arrived there. Oh, it's a long journey. My gosh. Uh, so ever since my provisional year. Very quiet. Milady. I'm new to this community. Basically. I know nobody except for my friend Hannah Laurie. And it took me believing in myself to say yes to one thing, and then somebody else saw me and they said yes to me and said, I believe you can do this. And every year since my provisional year, I took on a leadership role, going from Touch a Truck co-chair to advancement Co-Chair. Advancement. Chair. Nominations. Two nominations. Co-Chair, two nominations. Chair. Then to president elect and president. And that journey was challenging. It challenged me more ways than I can imagine, especially during Covid working in healthcare, I, I had a really hard time going to work, being present, being frankly really stressed out, working with Covid patients and hospital and outpatient settings, nursing home settings and having the energy to give my time to volunteer and in leadership. And I don't. I feel like I have a hard time just giving up on things. There are many times during this process, and nobody would have judged me if I just said, I need to take a step back because I'm burnt out and there is something like in my head that said, no, you have a purpose. You can make change in the league. People need you. And despite, like all the sleepless nights and meetings and going to work and just getting completely wiped out and still going home and being on my computer and writing emails and getting ready for all the things and volunteering outside of that, uh, I knew that I had a purpose and I had to keep going. And I don't regret that at all. Being president in this league is such an honor. There's so many women before me that, you know, you you think about and, you know, I talk to them and like, gosh, they're amazing women. And look what they were able to do. And the virtue of being in this position is that I instantly gained like 20 new friends in these sustainers like, they really, like, come to bat for me. And I can call any one of them and say, hey, I need to talk to you for coffee. Can you meet with me? And they would hands down say yes. So I want to come back to ask you some more questions about being president. But since you talked about building relationships in league, would you be willing to talk a little bit about the types of relationships that you've grown in the league, maybe mentorships or friendships? Yeah. I'd say there's been a handful of people that I got really close with, um, both past presidents and current members, like people that I kind of started with, like my my cohort. I'm the only one left from that cohort. Uh, the other two gals left within a year after our provisional time. A year or two. Uh, and to be very honest with you, for a good while, I didn't feel like I had friends in the league. Like there were people that I'd see volunteering and that I'd be, like, in committee with. But it's not like I would say, hey, let's let's go and do this thing together. There's probably like maybe two people that were like that, but, you know, they weren't in like my cohort. They kind of like phased out before I did, especially during Covid. There was a lot of people that phased out, uh, and I felt very lonely for a really long time. I remember one of the first in-person games that we had, uh, there was an icebreaker where it was about like, you know, deib like belonging and being a part of this organization. And they'd be asking us questions about, like what? Describe a time for me that you felt like you belonged. And I just sat very quiet. I'm like, I don't know if I have. No, because. To be honest with you, there's a lot of women in the league that are very well off. They come from really, uh, you know, fortunate backgrounds and, you know, they Have high paying jobs. They have a husband. They have kids. I had none of those things. Like I was still coming into my own and being different, having a wife. And I'm the only out person in the league. Uh, I still felt like I was one of the others. Were you always out in the league, or did you feel like you had to come out? I was always out. By the time I joined the league, I was already married. And that was like during a time where I had. Conversations with myself and being a little bit more brave, like at my job where I said, I don't want to be ashamed and afraid to be who I am. So yes, they all know that I had a wife. And it was very funny because these conversations of, you know, going to this volunteer and bring your husbands and they look at me or your wife. I'm like, okay, I guess I'm the only one. Have you felt like you were able to bring your whole self to the role of president? 100%, yeah. I feel like this year as president, I've never felt more myself than I have in any other year. And I attribute that to my current job. Uh, when I transitioned from an outpatient orthopedic to pelvic health, the women and the leadership in my work have created a space for me and intention that I can be myself and be a better version of myself. And this space that has been given to me to grow and be confident has overflowed into my confidence in the league, and I think that really shows. I hear a lot of women who can see that leadership in me and see how I talk and how I interact with people, and they are kind of mimicking that behavior. And that's exactly what I love to see, because I think of myself as someone that gives fully and, um, you know, with, with such grace and love and, and I want people to feel safe when they're with me. And I think that's starting to move other people to do the same thing. So you may feel like you've just answered this, but, um, do you feel like as president, you're leaving a legacy of any kind? And if you are, what is that? Oh, gosh. When I think of that, I think of actually a past president that I, I like to emulate and that's, uh, um, Michelle Wooten. She was a lot like somebody that I wanted to emulate. She was very thoughtful. She was not quick to come to conclusions. And I think people respected her in that way of being a president. President that, um, created space for for that. And she was very well respected of that. And I think, like, I'm taking that and putting my own spin on it, uh. And creating clear intentions that people don't have to be ashamed or afraid to be themselves. Because I'm coming to to this space wholly myself. And people accept that of me. I've never once heard anybody tell me or like through the grapevine that, um, that they, you know, don't like me because I'm gay or because, you know, I'm I'm coming from a different background. Um, or I do things differently. I think that there's a lot of respect and and even, like, encouragement for, for all of us to do the same. Because when we come as our whole selves, that's when we become great. What what is something that you hope continues on with the League into the future? I know that, like AJ is spearheading a lot of really great, um, initiatives. Where we stop pushing ourselves to do more and we inspire ourselves and others to do less well, because we all work and have kids and have, you know, parents or grandparents that we take care of and a million other things in our life that is taking up our time. And we have the league to be better versions of ourselves. And my hope is that we lean into that perspective of the league isn't something that we go to to do more work. It's some it's an organization of women who help each other to not just create change in the community, but also change ourselves. And that's where real impact comes, is when we can come into our volunteer life and our work life and our home life as better versions, because we learned that in the league. What would you want other people to know about Junior League? That maybe they don't. Uh, it's not like anything else out there. When you think of a volunteer organization, you know, you think of people who like they have one, cause you have the food bank, you have, uh, the Women's and Children's Alliance, you have the learning lab. Like they have one thing that they do really well. What's great about the league is that we change what we do. Well, we we create new opportunities depending on what the the vibe of the league is at the time. And we we pivot and we go and do something and maybe we create a project and then give that to the community, and then we see another need and we create a project and give that to the community. So we are seeing what is needed and we manifest the results of impact. Was there anything I should have asked you in this interview that I didn't? Let me think. Maybe not something that you should have asked, but just a thought that I've been considering when preparing for this interview is how my life story. As. Painful as a lot of it was. Made me who I am right now. It. It makes me feel that all the horrible stuff that I've gone through as a kid and the. This. Despite that, I pushed through and and changed my life because I didn't want to be in a situation where I was homeless and I didn't have enough to eat, and I didn't have money to get clothes or live in a house. And that pushed me to be more and do more. And love unconditionally. And God damn it, I did it. I'm 40 and I feel like everything I did had a purpose. And I go to work and I tell people like I change lives every single day so I can make an impact for others to feel better when they're filling out their worst. And the league is just a part of that story. I think that's a great place to end it. Um. It is. Do I need to say 9:24 p.m., January 22nd? We'll end the recording.

lines.line: # Introduction and Purpose of Interview

> Maggie introduces the oral history project at the Junior League of Boise. The interview aims to discuss Milady's life, especially her association with the Junior League. The oral history is part of a larger commemoration project supported by the Idaho Historical Society and America 250.
** introduction, oral history, Junior League, Boise, Idaho Historical Society, America 250 **
keywords: ** introduction, oral history, Junior League, Boise, Idaho Historical Society, America 250 **

** relationship, new beginnings, Lorena, identity **

metadata.title: Conclusion and Reflections
metadata.synopsis: The interview concludes with Milady reflecting on how her life experiences shaped her, her drive for change, and the interconnectedness of her personal and professional journey.

children.text: # Introduction and Purpose of Interview

> Maggie introduces the oral history project at the Junior League of Boise. The interview aims to discuss Milady's life, especially her association with the Junior League. The oral history is part of a larger commemoration project supported by the Idaho Historical Society and America 250.
** introduction, oral history, Junior League, Boise, Idaho Historical Society, America 250 **
# Milady's Personal Background

> Milady introduces herself and the interview takes a chronological approach to explore her life from her childhood in Spokane, Washington. She reflects on her family background, financial struggles, and early experiences.
** Milady, childhood, Spokane, family background, financial struggles **
# Early Childhood Experiences

> Milady shares her experiences growing up in a financially struggling household. Her father's self-employment as a massage therapist and her mother's work added to the home atmosphere where clients often visited.
** childhood, massage therapy, financial struggle, family **
# Impact of Family Separation

> Milady recounts her parents' divorce, remarriages, and the complexities arising from her father's second wife, whom she describes as a 'wicked stepmother.' She discusses the chaos and instability these changes brought to her life.
** divorce, remarriage, family dynamics, instability **
# Cultural and Personal Discoveries

> Milady discusses the influence of her mother’s second husband, a Native American. She shares experiences from Native American ceremonies and reflects on the constraints she felt due to gender-based cultural rules.
** Native American culture, gender roles, self-discovery **
# Overcoming Homelessness
> Milady shares the hardships of becoming homeless during her high school years, the impact on her education and life, and the resilience she had to muster during these challenging times.
** homelessness, high school, resilience, challenge **
# Parenting Dynamics and Sibling Experiences

> Milady discusses her father’s stance during her unstable housing situation. She highlights the psychological impact their environment had on her siblings, including her youngest brother's need for therapy.
** parenting, siblings, psychological impact, therapy **
# Exploring Identity and Family History

> Milady explains her family’s deep roots in the Mormon Church, how her parents distanced themselves due to personal beliefs, and her personal journey from Mormonism to exploring other belief systems.
** identity, Mormon Church, family history, belief systems **
# Shift from Mormonism and Family Dynamics
> Milady discusses her family’s transition from the Mormon faith, the blacklisting from community, and her mother’s exploration of paganism, impacting Milady’s feeling of being an outsider.
** faith transition, blacklisting, paganism, outsider **
# Childhood Food Insecurity
> Milady shares vivid memories of food insecurity during her childhood, the creative ways her family coped with scarcity, and how these experiences influenced her current attitudes towards food.
** food insecurity, childhood, coping mechanisms **
# Path to Higher Education
> Milady talks about her decision to attend Washington State University, the influence of mentors, and the challenges she faced as a first-generation college student.
** education, mentors, Washington State University, challenges **
# Career Aspirations in Healthcare
> Milady explains her journey in the field of physical therapy, detailing her attempts to advance in her career, and the resilience required to pursue her passion despite setbacks.
** career, physical therapy, resilience, healthcare **
# Personal Revelations and Relationship Changes

> Milady discusses her realization of being gay, the collapse of her marriage, and meeting her second partner. She shares the impact of societal views on her journey and how it shaped her future relationships.
** sexual orientation, marriage, relationships, societal impact **
# Challenges and Identity Acceptance

> Milady reflects on her journey of self-awareness regarding her sexual orientation, the gradual acceptance of her identity, and the personal and social challenges faced during her married life with a man.
** self-awareness, identity, challenges, acceptance **
# Embracing Change and Personal Growth
> This chapter discusses Milady's pathway to embracing her sexual orientation, the realization of her true self in college, and the subsequent internal conflicts she managed while married.
** change, personal growth, college, conflicts **
# Life with Lorena and New Beginnings
> Milady recounts meeting Lorena, their evolving relationship, and how their connection motivated her to improve her life conditions and fully embrace her identity.
** relationship, new beginnings, Lorena, identity **
# Involvement with the Junior League
> Milady talks about joining the Junior League of Boise, her initial shyness, and how she grew into leadership roles, helping to organize and run major events.
** Junior League, leadership, events, involvement **
# Adapting Events Amidst a Pandemic
> This chapter covers how the Junior League adapted its activities during the pandemic, the shift to virtual interactions, and the impact on volunteer initiatives.
** pandemic, adaptation, virtual events, Junior League **
# Reflections on Leadership and Legacy
> Milady shares her journey to becoming league president, the challenges faced, and her vision for a supportive and inclusive organization that fosters growth and change.
** leadership, presidency, legacy, vision **
# Inspiration and Future of the League
> Milady reflects on the impact she hopes to make as president and her desire to foster a supportive league environment. She emphasizes the importance of adaptation and addressing community needs.
** inspiration, league future, adaptation, support **
# Conclusion and Reflections
> The interview concludes with Milady reflecting on how her life experiences shaped her, her drive for change, and the interconnectedness of her personal and professional journey.
** conclusion, reflections, life experiences, change **
